heroTitle: Store, sync, and share files across devices anywhere
cardOneTitle: Easily share content with your audiences
cardOneSubtitle: Need to share news, event documents, videos or educational material to your audiences without relying on centralized platforms that are easily blocked? With Ouisync, you can quickly upload media and share Read-only access to content via QR codes, shortlinks, or other messaging platforms. Ouisync is 100% free and open source, and will never share or sell your data to third parties.
cardTwoTitle: Quickly share files with a friend
cardTwoSubtitle: Share a Ouisync repository using Write permissions to allow multiple users to add or edit files within a repository, even in conditions of limited connectivity. Keep data in sync between your personal devices, collaborate on team projects, and create group photo albums. All data in Ouisync is end-to-end encrypted for your peace of mind.
cardThreeSubtitle: Want to securely back up your data on other devices and make it inaccessible to anyone else? Share your Ouisync repositories in Blind mode. Your friend offers you their spare computer for storage of your data? Let your friends be your cloud! It takes just a couple of taps in Ouisync to securely store your data in Blind mode and you never again have to worry about losing your phone - your data is safely stored for whenever you need it.
infoOneContent: Data (including metadata such as file name, size or location) is encrypted both in transit and at rest, and can be shared as read/write, read-only, or blind.
infoTwoContent: Ouisync users are able to share files despite conditions of limited or no internet connectivity, when the centralised servers most apps rely upon may not be reachable.
infoThreeContent: Easily create encrypted repositories for your data and then distribute the link to friends, audiences, or between devices for effective, decentralized publication and/or backup storage.
footerEQStatement1: – a not-for-profit Canadian company creating decentralized internet services in support of a more equal and equitable network. Our solutions are open source, battle proven and developed in mind of our
footerEQStatement2: Everyday, they enable freedom of association for millions of people online.
aboutSubtitle: Ouisync is a private and resilient solution for syncing files and folders with your devices, contacts and groups.
aboutFeatureOneSubtitle: Simply install and quickly create files and folders to sync and share with trusted devices, contacts and/or groups.
aboutFeatureTwoSubtitle: Ouisync uses an innovative, synchronous, peer-to-peer design that allows users to access and share files and folders whether or not your device can connect to the internet.
aboutFeatureThreeSubtitle: End-to-end encrypted files and folders - both in transit and at rest - secured by established, state-of-the art protocols.
aboutFeatureFourSubtitle: No in-app purchases, no subscriptions, no ads, and no tracking!
aboutFeatureFiveSubtitle: Create repositories that can be shared as read-write, read-only, or blind (you store files for others, but cannot access them).
aboutFeatureSixSubtitle: Ouisync's source code is 100% free and open source software, now and forever. All code can be found on
statusP: **July 2023:** We are soft launching the first stable release of Ouisync this month! Please note that the app is under active development, and as such some features and functionality may not work as expected. We encourage users to report bugs and request new features [via Github](https://github.com/equalitie/ouisync-app)
teamSubtitle: Full bios for the Ouisync team and the rest of the staff at eQualitie are available [on the eQualitie website](https://equalitie.org/about-us).
getInTouchSubtitle: For support questions, press and media, and feedback, you can contact the Ouisync team at: support [at] ouisync [dot] net.
communitySubtitle: We'd love your support in making Ouisync as useful and accessible as possible. Ouisync is a completely Free/Libre/Open Source project, and there are many ways to contribute!
sourceCodeSubtitle: If you are interested in Ouisync's source code please check the following Git repositories:
contactUsSubtitle: To contact the Ouisync team, send an email to support [at] ouisync [dot] net.
socialMediaTitle: Follow us and join the conversation on your preferred social media platform:

faqContent010101: A _**repository**_ is simply a place where you can store and share your files and folders securely, using **Ouisync**. You can think of it as a root folder, or even a safe, that will contain other folders and files that you want to share with your peers.
faqTitle0102: Where can I see my repositories?
faqContent010201: When you open the **Ouisync app**, after the onboarding screens you will see the main screen listing all the _**repositories**_ you have created. Initially this screen will be empty, but as you begin creating _**repositories**_, they will be listed here.
faqTitle0201: How can I create new repositories?
faqContent020101: To create a new _**repository**_, tap the  **+** button:
faqContent020104: When creating a _**repository**_ you need to decide if you want to use the _**Ouisync cache server**_.
faqContent020105: Generally speaking, if you want to sync your data with peers or devices that are online at the same time as you, then the server option may not be necessary.
faqContent020106: But when you want to sync some data with your peers who are not online at the same time as you, using the server may be helpful. [Learn more](#privacy-and-security).
faqTitle0202: How can I add files and folders to my repository?
faqContent020201: That's easy. Tap on the _**repository name**_.  This will take you to the _**repository**_ contents screen where you will see a **Plus** button:
faqContent020202: This will open a small window where you can choose whether to create a folder within that _**repository**_ or add files to it
faqContent020203: If you decide to create a **new folder** this dialog box will allow you to do it:
faqContent020204: Alternatively, you can import files into one of the existing folders.
faqTitle0301: What does it mean to 'import' a repository?
faqContent030101: To import a _**repository**_ means that you want to recreate on your device a _**repository**_ that a peer has [shared](#sharing) with you.
faqContent030102: You start with the **+** button and then select **Import**:
faqContent030103: Tapping on the **Import repository** button will bring you to this screen:
faqContent030104: Here you have two options - you can scan a **QR code** generated by your peer or copy and paste the **link** your peer has sent you into the provided field. The **link** looks like this:
faqContent030105: After you either copy and paste the **link** you received from your peers or scan the **QR code**, you will see this screen.
faqContent030106: On this screen, you need to give your imported _**repository**_ a name and decide if you want to make use of the [Ouisync server](#privacy-and-security). You can leave the name the same as the one suggested on the screen, or you can change it. Tapping on the Import button will complete the process of importing a _**repository**_.
faqContent030107: The imported _**repository**_ will appear on your screen and after a short while all its content will be imported into your device.  In other words, the Ouisync _**repositories**_ on your devices will sync their data with the _**Ouisync repositories**_ on your peer’s device.  This will happen automatically, without the need for you to perform any further action.
faqTitle0302: I have imported a repository - what should I do next?
faqContent030201: If your device is connected to the device that shared the _**repository**_ with you, then syncing will start automatically.
faqContent030202: To verify that devices in question are indeed connected, please go to the app **Settings**, and then scroll down to the **Peers** section. Tapping on it will open a screen that will show you a list of devices that are **Connected** to your device.
faqContent030203: If the device whose _**repository**_ you imported  has status **Connected**, you don't need to perform any additional action to make the syncing start.
faqContent030205: You can monitor the speed of data synchronisation by viewing the upload and dowload speeds to the right of the _**repository**_ name
faqContent030206: or you can go to **Repository Settings** screen and view the percentage of data synced (upper right hand corner).
faqContent030207: If your devices are connected to each other, every time a person who has the **write** access to a _**repository**_ changes its content (add a file, delete, rename etc), the shared _**repositories**_ will automatically sync with each other. When syncing is complete, you and your peer will have identical data in your respective _**repositories**_.
faqTitle0303: How can I know when the syncing of my repositories is completed?
faqContent030301: During the syncing, you will see these symbols next to the _**repository**_ name.
faqContent030302: Once the **syncing** is complete, you will see the **green check** icon next to the _**repository name**_. This means that you now have on your device the exact copy of the _**repository**_ that your peer has shared with you.
faqTitle0401: Can I delete my Ouisync repositories?
faqContent040101: Yes. You can do that by going to **repository settings** screen and tapping on the **Delete** button.
faqTitle0402: What happens to the contents when I delete a repository?
faqContent040201: When you delete a _**repository**_ on your device, this deletion is not propagated to your peer’s devices – they will still have all their _**repositories**_.
faqContent040202: If you delete a _**repository**_ that you have **not shared** with any other device or peer, then your Ouisync data is deleted.
faqContent040203: You can create a new _**repository**_ and upload the same files into it - if you have them somewhere else on your devices.
faqContent040204: If your files have only been stored in a Ouisync _**repository**_ which you did not share with anyone, and you delete that _**repository**_, your data will be lost.
faqTitle0403: I have shared my repository then deleted it in error.  Can I undo this?
faqContent040301: If you delete a _**repository**_ that you have already **shared** with another device or peer, you can re-import it from them back into your primary device.
faqContent040302: If you shared a _**repository**_ with **write** permissions, your peers can generate a link and send it back to you with the same permissions.
faqContent040303: If you created a _**repository**_ and you shared it with a **read** permissions, your peers can generate only a **read** link to send back to you.
faqContent040304: The kind of access a user can have is **encoded in the link**.
faqContent040306: _A teacher creates a Ouisync _**repository**_, uploads the study material into it and shares it with their students in **read only** mode._
faqContent040307: _Then a small accident happens and the the teacher's _**repository**_ gets deleted.   The teacher now cannot share new study material with their students._
faqContent040308: _Students are able to share **links** with their teacher, but... only with **read** access.  The teacher can import the _**repository**_ and the data will sync with their students' _**repositories**_._
faqContent040309: _The teacher, having received the **read access link** from their student, also has only **read access**.  In this situation, nobody has a **write access** to the _**repository**_ – everybody can read it, but nobody can edit it._
faqContent040310: _However, If that teacher generates a **write link** prior to deleting the _**repository**_, and stores it somewhere safely, then they are able to re-import the same _**repository**_ using that **link**, even after they deleted it on their device._
faqContent040311: _The data from their students' _**repositories**_ will sync with the teacher’s new _**repository**_.  That way the teacher’s _**repository**_ will contain all the data from students’ _**repositories**_ and they will also have the **write access**._
faqContent040312: The type of link used to import a _**repository**_, determines the type of access to that _**repository**_.
faqContent040313: **To be able to retrieve data from a deleted _**repository**_, a user needs two elements: the type of link they require to have the access to the data, and a copy of the data itself on another device.  With these two elements, any _**repository**_ can be re-imported.**
faqTitle0501: How can I share my repository with my peers (or my other devices)?
faqContent050101: You can do this by tapping the _**repository**_ **Settings** menu and then tapping the **Share** symbol:
faqContent050102: This will open a small window on the bottom part of your screen.
faqContent050103: Here you need to decide which **permission** (or **access mode**) to give to your peers and whether you want to share the _**repository**_ using the **link** or the **QR code**.
faqTitle0502: How do I decide which permissions to select when sharing a repository?
faqContent050201: When you share a _**repository**_ with your peers, you decide if you want to give them the **write**, **read** or **blind** permission to your repository.
faqContent050202: When you import a _**repository**_ from your peers, they decide what type of access they will give you.
faqContent050204: If you want your peers to be able to add files, delete them, rename or move them within the _**repository**_ that you shared with them, then you need to share your _**repository**_ with **Write** permission.
faqContent050205: An example use case for this level of permissions: _sharing photos with friends and family, or working collaboratively on a project._
faqContent050206: Everybody who has the **write** permission will be able to add or delete files or move them around, and their actions will sync with all users who have imported that same _**repository**_.
faqContent050208: If you want your peers to only be able to read the _**repository**_ contents, then select **Read permissions**. This means they will be able to open the files and read them, but they won't be able to add new files to your shared _**repository**_, nor move or delete any files from it.
faqContent050209: An example use case would be when you want to _share the information regarding an event, news items or products, or perhaps you are a teacher sharing some educational content with your students, etc_. In all these cases you want the recipients to be able to read the contents but not change them.
faqContent050211: This level of permissions can be useful when you want to [securely store your data](#secure-data-storage) in a **Ouisync** _**repository**_. This means that the person or device with whom you shared your _**repository**_ as **Blind** won't be able to open the files to read them, nor make any changes to them. Learn more about [Blind repositories](#secure-data-storage).
faqTitle0503: To share by Link or QR code?
faqContent050301: Here you need to decide if you want to share a repository using a link or QR code.
faqContent050302: If the peer (or device) with whom you want to share a _**repository**_ is nearby, you can generate the **QR code** for them to scan.
faqContent050303: After that, they need to tap on the **Import** button.
faqContent050304: This action will import a copy of your _**repository**_ into your peer's device, including all the files and folders within it.
faqContent050305: If your peer is not nearby, you can share your _**repositories**_ by generating a **link**.
faqContent050306: The **Copy link** button will copy the generated link which you can paste into a message and securely send to your peers.
faqContent050307: The **Share link** button will offer you a choice of apps you can use to send the link.
faqContent050308: Note: it is important to send links securely because anybody who has a link will be able to access the files in your repository.
faqContent050309: When they receive the **link**,  your peers will need to go to the app home page, and then tap the **plus** button to start the **Import** _**repository**_ process.  They will need to copy and paste the **link** into the provided field and proceed with the _**repository**_ [import](#import).
faqContent050310: If a peer receives a **link** via a messaging app on their Android smartphone, the **link** will be tappable.  When tapped, it will open the **import** _**repository**_ screen, where the _**repository**_ name will be prefilled but can be changed by the peer.  Tapping on the **Import** button will complete the process.
faqTitle0504: Can my peers re-share my links?
faqContent050401: Yes. They can generate the **links** with the same permissions they had in the original **link** that they received from you, or lower.
faqContent050402: This means that if a person has received a **link** to import a _**repository**_ with **Write** permissions they are able to generate the same kind of link and re-share the same _**repository**_ with other people. They can also generate links for the same _**repository**_ but with lower permissions (**Read** or **Blind**).
faqContent050403: If they imported a **_repository_** with **Read** permissions only, they can share it with others as **Read** or **Blind**. If they imported your _**repository**_ as **Blind**, they can only share it as **Blind**.
faqContent050404: This is similar to what happens when you send somebody an email - they can forward that email to other people.
faqTitle0505: Can I revoke the link I have shared with a peer?
faqContent050502: If an undesired peer gains access to your _**repository**_, the Ouisync app cannot block them, because the app doesn’t know which peer is desired and which one might not be.
faqContent050503: As a user, you might know because your peers may tell you, but the app only knows who imported the shared _**repositories**_ and will sync the data between those peers.
faqContent050504: To avoid undesired peers gaining access to your _**repositories**_, we recommend that you share the important Ouisync **links** using secure means of communication and only with people you trust.
faqContent050505: Our roadmap includes the password protected links and adding the trusted users features, which will further improve security when sharing Ouisync _**repositories**_.
faqTitle0506: What can I do if I shared a link with a peer but I don’t want them to have access to that repository any more?
faqContent050601: Currently, the Ouisync app does not provide a one click solution for revoking the _**repository**_ access.  As a workaround, this is what you can do:
faqContent050602: - create a new _**repository**_, which you will not share with the same person,
faqContent050603: - replicate the data from the previously shared _**repository**_ to the new one,
faqContent050604: - if there are any files or folders that remain in the previously shared _**repository**_, delete them,
faqContent050605: - share the new _**repository**_ only with people who you want to give access to and ask them not to share it further.
faqTitle0507: I want to change the type of access to my repository that I gave to my peers.  How can I do that?
faqContent050701: Currently, the Ouisync app does not distinguish between the _**repository**_ creator and the peer who imported a _**repository**_ if they both have the same permissions (in this case: **write**).
faqContent050702: In previous releases, once a _**repository**_ has been imported it wasn’t possible to change its **access** permissions without deleting it, and importing it again with a different permission or **access** type.
faqContent050703: From release 0.9.0, if a peer possesses appropriate links, they are able to change the type of access they have for a _**repository**_.
faqContent050704: This can be done from the _**repository**_ **Security** page, by clicking on the blue link _**“Reset repository access using a share token”**_.
faqContent050705: Let’s take a look at an example.
faqContent050706: On this screenshot, the icon next to the green check indicates that this _**repository**_ can be edited, and the user has a **write** permissions for it.  Tapping on the three dot menu on the right, will open the _**repository**_ **Settings** page.  Tap on **Security** to enter the  _**repository**_ **Security** page.
faqContent050707: Then, please tap on the **blue link**.  This will open the following screen
faqContent050708: The upper part of this screen shows the current Repository name and access type.
faqContent050709: To change the **access type**, you need to have a link that encodes the **access type** to which you want to change the current one.
faqContent050710: Once you enter the new link in its field,
faqContent050712: you will see that the field Token access type acquires a value - in this example: Read.
faqContent050713: When you return to the main page of the repository whose access type you modified, you will see that it has a read icon next to its name - indication that the access type has indeed been changed.
faqContent050714: In summary: all the information on these screens serves to let you know that, for the given _**repository**_, you are changing the access type from **write** to **read**.  You can also change the access type from **write** to **blind**, or from **blind** or **read** to **write** - all subject to you possessing the appropriate **links**.
faqSectionTitle06: Using Blind Repositories for Secure Data Storage
faqContent060101: When sharing a _**repository**_, you can decide to share it with [Blind](#blind) permission.
faqContent060102: This means that it can be imported into another device and its purpose is simply to securely store data on that device.
faqContent060103: _Note: if your **blind** _**repository**_ is online at the same time as your other _**repositories**_, it will sync with your original _**repository**_._
faqContent060104: In other words, your **Blind** _**repository**_ will receive any updates that you make to your data.  If you rename, copy, move or delete files in your original _**repository**_, they are going to be automatically renamed, copied, moved or deleted in your **Blind** _**repository**_ as well.
faqTitle0602: Why is it called Secure Data Storage and  not simply Data Backup?
faqContent060201: Typically, if you want to back up your data, the backups would be created by some utility program, and they would be timestamped.  Thus, if you needed to, you would be able to go back to the previous backed up version.
faqContent060202: This is not the case with **Ouisync Blind** _**repositories**_.
faqContent060203: Currently, **Ouisync app** doesn’t create timestamped backups that contain previous versions of data, so you cannot go back to the version of _**repository**_ you had two weeks ago.
faqContent060204: This feature is on our roadmap, but for the time being your **blind** _**repository**_ will contain all the data that your original _**repository**_ contained when they last connected.
faqContent060205: As soon as they reconnect, their data will sync, and they will be the exact replica of each other.
faqTitle0603: What are my Blind repositories useful for?
faqContent060301: Examples of when they may be useful:
faqContent060302: When your primary device is _**damaged, lost or stolen**_ or when Ouisync _**repositories**_ in your primary device are _**accidentally (or deliberately) deleted**_
faqContent060303: The following two sections describe the steps you need to perform to successfully store and retrieve your data from a **blind** Ouisync _**repository**_
faqTitle0604: How can I store my data securely using Ouisync Blind repositories?
faqContent060401: To do that, please follow these steps:
faqContent060402: **Step 1:** Create a _**repository**_ in a normal way, and add data that you want to store to it.
faqContent060403: **Step 2:** Go to _**repository**_ settings, and tap the **Share** button.  Select **Write** and copy the link.  (For more details see section [Sharing](#sharing))
faqContent060404: **Step 3:** Paste the **link** and keep it somewhere safe.  You will need it to retrieve the data from this _**repository**_ later on.  _**Note: if you lose it, there is no way to retrieve the data.  We do not keep your links anywhere on our systems**_.
faqContent060405: **Step 4:** Go back to _**repository**_ settings, tap the **Share** button and now select **Blind**, and either copy the link, share it or generate the QR code.
faqContent060406: **Step 5:** Using a spare device (which we also refer to as storage device), [import](#import) this _**repository**_ with access mode **Blind**
faqContent060407: That's it. You data is now securely stored.
faqContent060408: What this means is that in your spare device (or your friend’s spare device) you have created an exact replica of the original _**repository**_ that resides on your primary device, but nobody can access it, because it is **blind**, ie it doesn’t have either **read** or **write** access.
faqTitle0605: How can I retrieve data from my Blind repositories?
faqContent060501: To retrieve data from your **Blind** repository, you need to have a valid link for it (which you have generated  before).
faqContent060502: Let’s look at two different scenarios:
faqContent060503: The original repository on the primary device doesn’t exist any more, repository is re-imported from the storage device
faqContent060504: To achieve this, please perform the following steps:
faqContent060505: **Step 1:** Make sure that the device containing your **Blind** _**repositories**_ is connected to your primary device via internet or wi-fi.
faqContent060506: **Step 2:** Locate the **write** link for this _**repository**_ (which you created and stored in steps 2 and 3 above).
faqContent060507: **Step 3:** As you don't have the original repository any more (maybe through loss of device or deletion of data), please open the **Ouisync app** on your primary or new device, and tap the **plus** button to **Import** the _**repository**_ in a normal way.
faqContent060508: **Step 4:** Paste the retrieved **write link**  in the provided field and tap the **Import** button
faqContent060509: **Step 5:** In a short while, the newly imported _**repository**_ will sync with the **Blind** _**repository**_ and will have an exact copy of the data stored in the **blind** _**repository**_.
faqContent060510: Retrieving data from Blind repositories by modifying the access type in the storage device
faqContent060511: From the version 0.9.0, it is also possible to retrieve data from a blind repository in the storage device itself.  The steps to perform are described in the section about [modifying access type for a _**repository**_](#modify-access)
faqContent060512: After the repository was re-imported into your primary device, or its data becomes available through change of access type in the storage device, all the changes you make to the _**repository**_ on your primary device will, once again, be synced with your **Blind** _**repository**_, as long as devices remain connected .
faqContent060513: If you shared that repository with other peers, and they make changes to it, their changes will sync with the blind repository too if they are connected to each other.
faqContent060514: If you (or other peers) add files to your primary _**repository**_, that addition will be propagated to your **Blind** _**repository**_ too. If you (or other peers) delete any files in your primary _**repository**_, then that deletion will be propagated too.
faqContent060515: _**Note:** if you lose your **Write** link for your securely stored **Blind**  _**repository**_, and you did not share that _**repository**_ with anybody else, you won't be able to retrieve your stored data._
faqTitle0701: What happens if my peers and myself upload the same file into Ouisync?
faqContent070101: **Ouisync** is capable of handling two different scenarios.
faqContent070102: Peers who are online at the same time
faqContent070103: If you and one or more peers upload a file (for example Billy.jpg) that already exists in a **Ouisync** _**repository**_ , the app will detect this situation and ask if you want to keep both files or replace one of them.
faqContent070104: If you decide to keep both of them, the new one will have (1) added to the name and you will see both files on your **Ouisync** screen. If you decide to replace the existing one, it will be overwritten.
faqContent070105: Peers who are offline at the time of uploading the files
faqContent070106: If you and one or more peers upload the same file (for example Free_Test-Data_1MB.docx) when you are offline and therefore not able to sync files immediately, your files will sync when you are able to connect to your peers again.
faqContent070107: In case of two or more files bearing the same name, **Ouisync** will detect this situation and it will add random string at the end of the name of each file - to distinguish between them.  You can then view them and decide which one to keep, whether to rename them etc.
faqTitle0702: What happens if I delete files in my repository?
faqContent070201: File deletion is propagated to all replicas in existence - which means the same file that you deleted will be automatically deleted in the _**repositories**_ of all the peers who have imported your _**repository**_.
faqContent070202: Equally, if your peers delete any files in any of the _**repositories**_ that they have imported from you, their file deletions will be propagated to your device too. It works both ways -i.e.: _**repositories**_ shared  with **Write** permissions will sync with each other, including all file operations.
faqContent070203: File deletion is only possible in _**repositories**_ for which you have the **write** access.  The file options available in those _**repositories**_ are:
faqContent070204: The file options for _**repositories**_ to which you have only a **read** access are:
faqContent070205: When you only have a **read** access, you cannot rename, move nor delete files.
faqTitle0703: What happens if I rename files in my repository?
faqContent070301: If you rename files in your _**repository**_, the new file name will be propagated to the _**repositories**_ of all the peers that you shared your _**repository**_ with.
faqTitle0704: Can I move my files from one repository to another?
faqContent070401: Yes. Moving files from one _**repository**_ to another is possible.
faqContent070402: To move a file to a different _**repository**_, tap on the three dot menu next to its name.  This will open the file options menu, where you can select **Move**.
faqContent070403: Next, select the file that you want to move.
faqContent070404: After that you need to select the destination where you want to move the file to.  This can be a folder within the same _**repository**_
faqContent070405: or it can be a location in another _**repository**_.
faqContent070406: We also provide the way to move **multiple files** together. To do this, you need to first tap on the **Select** button
faqContent070407: This will allow you to make a selection of multiple files.
faqContent070408: Next, you need to select the destination folder or _**repository**_.
faqContent070409: And then tap on the **Move** button.  This completes the process.
faqTitle0705: Can I share individual files with my peers?
faqContent070502: On Android, within the Ouisync file options, there is also the option to share them.
faqContent070503: Like in other apps, when you tap this option, another window will open offering you a choice of apps you can use to share this individual file.
faqContent070504: You can select any messaging app to send this file to your peers.
faqContent070505: However, caution... If you do that, the file is not shared within the Ouisync any more, and therefore it is not protected by the Ouisync encryption.
faqContent070506: Within the Ouisync app itself, currently it is only possible to share whole repositories, and not individual files and folders.
faqTitle0706: Can I share files from my Android device into the Ouisync app?
faqContent070601: Yes.  When you see the familiar share symbol
faqContent070602: on your android device, tapping it normally brings up a window that offers you different apps you can use to share that file with.  One of those apps is the Ouisync app as well.
faqContent070603: Tapping the Ouisync icon here, opens the Ouisync app screens, where you can choose the location where you want your shared file to reside in within the Ouiysnc app.
faqTitle0707: Can I access the files in my Ouisync repositories from another Android app?
faqContent070702: Ouisync can be accessed from other android apps.
faqContent070703: For example: let’s imagine you want to attach a file that you have in a Ouisync _**repository**_ to a message in Signal.  You can do that as follows:
faqContent070704: Create a new message and tap on the plus button (or a paperclip) to attach a file to it.  This will open a screen where you can select a file, or an app.
faqContent070705: Select Ouisync.  Then you will see a screen that lists your exisitng Ouisync _**repositories**_.
faqContent070706: Select a _**repository**_ to view the files within it, and then select a file you want to attach to your Signal message.
faqContent070707: That’s it.  You can now send a file that you imported directly from the Ouisync app.
faqTitle0801: Are my files stored on a server? Why?
faqContent080101: Yes. They are stored fully encrypted in transport as well as at rest and are **not** readable by the server.
faqContent080102: The  purpose of this temporary server storage is to facilitate file syncing between peers when they are not online at the same time or when they cannot establish a direct peer-to-peer connection.
faqContent080103: If you want to share a _**repository**_ with a peer who is not online at the moment, your _**repository**_  data will be stored encrypted on the server and when your peer comes  online and connects to the server (or to your device) the files  from the stored _**repository**_ will sync with the files in your peer's repository.
faqContent080104: The server is **zero-knowledge** and it's purpose is to ensure reachability by temporarily storing data and providing it to those who posses the corresponding **token link**.
faqTitle0802: Is there a way to opt-out?
faqContent080202: When creating or importing a _**repository**_, you can decide to use this option, or to opt out of it.
faqContent080203: If you want to change your decision later on, you can do that in the _**repository**_ options screen.
faqTitle0803: What happens when Use Cache Server is ON?
faqContent080301: If this option is ON, your data is stored on the cache server for a short period of time.  The stored data is fully encrypted in transport as well as at rest and is not readable by the server.
faqContent080302: When your peer comes online at the time when you are offline, they will connect to the server and  the stored _**repository**_ will sync with your peer's _**repository**_.
faqTitle0804: What happens when Use Cache Server is OFF?
faqContent080401: When this toggle is off your data is not stored on cache servers.  This means that you will be able to sync your _**repositories**_ only with peers  or devices that are online at the same time as you.
faqContent080402: If you initially decide to **Use Cache Server** your _**repositories**_ are going to be temporarily stored on the **Cache Server**.  If you then decide to opt out and not use this option any more, your previously stored _**repositories**_ will be deleted from the server.  You can opt in or out as many times as you want.
faqTitle0805: Why don’t I see the Cache Server toggle?
faqContent080501: You will see this toggle only for _**repositories**_ for which you have the **write access**.  For _**repositories**_ with read or blind access, this toggle is hidden.
faqTitle0806: How can I connect to the server?
faqContent080601: If you select **Use Cache Server**  when you create a _**repository**_ ,your device will automatically connect to the cache server. You don't need to perform any additional actions.
faqTitle0807: How long is my data kept on the cache server?
faqContent080701: We keep your data on our **Cache Server** for 24 hours after the last syncing activity.  This means that the server will help sync _**repositories**_ with your peers who are online at least once a day.
faqContent080801: Currently, the size of _**repositories**_ synced via our **Cache Server** is limited to 1GB.  If you try to sync more than 1GB of data, the server will sync 1GB and then stop.  We provide this service free of charge and have imposed the _**repository**_ limits to prevent abuse.
faqTitle0809: Where is the Cache Server and who runs it?
faqContent080901: The **Ouisync** servers are physically located in Canada and are managed by eQualitie.
faqTitle0810: Can I set up my own server to use with my peers?
faqContent081001: Not yet.  We are working on it and in the near future you will be able to set up your own **Cache Server** to use with your peers.
faqTitle0811: What private data does Ouisync use/store?
faqContent081101: **Ouisync** uses the IP addresses of your devices to be able to connect you with your peers in the peer-to-peer network. We don't store those IP addresses anywhere on our systems. We don't keep any other user data.
faqTitle0812: How can I lock my repositories when I'm not actively using them?
faqContent081201: To lock your _**repositories**_ when not actively working on them, tap on the **Lock** button.
faqContent081202: To unlock them, first you need to tap on the _**repository**_ name.
faqContent081203: If your _**repository**_ is protected by password or biometrics, enter the password or scan your fingerprint when prompted. Otherwise, just tap on the **Unlock** button and continue to work on your repository.
faqContent081204: Note: Locked _**repositories**_ are not accessible in file explorer on your desktop machines. To be able to access them, you first need to unlock them via Ouisync app screens.
faqContent081205: If on your Windows machine you notice that some _**repositories**_ are not displayed in file explorer, please check within the app to make sure they are unlocked.
faqContent081206: If you are still not able to see them, please check our [Ouisync on Windows](#windows) or our [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting) section.
faqTitle0901: Do I have to set up a password (or biometrics) to protect my repositories?
faqContent090101: There is no requirement to protect your _**repositories**_ either by **password** or by **biometrics**.
faqContent090102: Whether you decide to do it depends on the sensitivity of data that you store in _**Ouisync repositories**_ and habitual usage of your devices.
faqContent090103: For storing and sharing photos of your cat, maybe a **password** is not necessary.  But for storing more sensitive personal data, we recommend setting up the **password (or biometrics) protection**.
faqTitle0902: How can I add local password for a repository?
faqContent090201: You can add **local password** to a _**respository**_ at the time of creation or import by selecting the **Use local password** option.
faqContent090202: You can also do this later on by going to _**repository**_ **Security screen** and selecting the same option.
faqContent090301: When **local password** is set up, you will be required to type it in every time you want to unlock your _**repository**_ or when you want to enter the _**repository**_ **Security screen**.
faqContent090302: To avoid having to type it so many times, you can use the **Remember password** option. This option works in a way similar to the ‘remember password’ option on websites.
faqContent090303: Activating this option means that you will not be required to enter the password when unlocking the _**repository**_, but you will still need to do it when you want to enter the **Security screen**.
faqTitle0904: Can I have different passwords for different repositories?
faqContent090402: You could have a _**repository**_ that you use for work purposes, and it can be protected by a **password**. On the same device, you could also have a _**repository**_ that you share with friends or family, and you could protect that one with a different **password**.
faqTitle0905: Can I have different passwords for the same repository on different devices?
faqContent090502: For example - you have a _**repository**_ that you share between your Android phones and your Windows desktop.  You can have one **password** for that _**repository**_ on your Android phone, and a different password for the same _**repository**_ on your Windows machine.
faqTitle0906: What happens if I lose my repository password?
faqContent090601: The first thing to know is that we don’t store your passwords, and because Ouisync is a peer-to-peer app, there isn’t a mechanism to reset your password that you may be used to in apps that have central servers.
faqContent090602: There are a few different scenarios that may play out if you lose or forget your password.
faqContent090603: Losing the password for a _**repository**_ that you shared with your peers
faqContent090604: If you lose a password, it can happen that you are not able to unlock the _**repository**_ that you previously locked.
faqContent090605: The simplest thing you can do in this case is to delete the repository and import it again from your peers, if their repositories have permissions that you require.
faqContent090606: However, if you wold prefer to avoid unecessary network traffic, instead of re-importing the repository, you can [reset the access type for it](#modify-access).
faqContent090607: When you try to tap the Unlock button, or when you want to enter the repository security page, the app will present a dialog box for you to enter the password.
faqContent090608: Of course, if you have lost your password, you won't be able to enter it.  Instead, you can tap the blue link “_I don’t have a local password for this **repository**_”.  This will open the [Reset _**repository**_ access screen](#modify-access).
faqContent090609: If you have shared the _**repository**_ in question, you can ask your peers to resend you the link so you can complete the reset procedure. If you need help, our step by step guide is available in this [section](#modify-access).
faqContent090610: Once you complete that process, your _**repository**_ access will be reset, and there will be no password set for it.  You can set it up again, or you can use your _**repository**_ without the password protection.
faqContent090611: Losing a password for a _**repository**_ that you didn’t share, but you have generated and stored its write link
faqContent090612: If you lose the password for a _**repository**_ that you did not share with others, but you have generated and stored its **write** link, you can use that link to reset the _**repository**_ access in the same way as described above.
faqContent090613: Losing a password that you didn’t share and for which you have not generated and stored a link
faqContent090614: And, finally, if you lose a password for a _**repository**_ that you did not share with others and for which you did not previously generate and store the link, you may lose the access to the Ouisync data in that _**repository**_.
faqTitle0907: Do I need to share the repository passwords with my peers?
faqContent090702: The peer who is sharing a _**repository**_ can protect their own replica by their own password, and the peer who is importing that _**repository**_ can protect their own replica, on their device, with their own password.
faqTitle0908: How can I change a password for one repository?
faqContent090801: This can be done in the **Security screen** of your _**repository**_.  To get there, go into _**repository**_ **settings** and then **Security** option.
faqContent090802: In the fields provided you can type and retype the new password, then tap **Update**.
faqTitle0909: How can I delete a password for one repository?
faqContent090901: **Local password** can be deleted in the _**repository**_ **Security screen**, by moving the **Use local password** toggle to **OFF** position.
faqTitle0910: Why is it called 'local password' and not simply 'password'?
faqContent091001: A **local password** is a password set up only for your own device. You don't need to share it with your peers. They can set up their own passwords to protect the shared **Ouisync** _**repositories**_ on their own devices.
faqContent091002: This is different to what you may be used to.  For example, if you want to share a word document with a friend, and that document is password protected, you will need to give your friend the password so they can open the document you shared.
faqContent091003: But in the case of the **Ouisync** _**repositories**_, what gives a user access to a _**repository**_ is not the password, but the **link** shared.
faqContent091004: The purpose of **password protection** is to prevent access to the **Ouisync** _**repositories**_ if your device falls into the hands of undesired third parties.
faqContent091005: If you set up the **password protection** and you lock your **Ouisync** _**repositories**_, if any third party gains access to your device they won't be able to share your _**repositories**_ with anybody else.
faqContent091006: Or rather, they are able to share it only with **Blind** permission, which means that they cannot access your data.
faqContent091007: If you don't have the **password (or biometrics) protection**, and your _**repositories**_ are not locked, then any third party that gains access to your device can also access your data and share it or modify it.
faqContent091008: In summary: **Ouisync passwords** protect _**repositories**_ on one device - and to protect the **Ouisync** contents **passwords** need to be set up on each device.
faqTitle0911: Can I secure my repositories using Biometrics?
faqContent091101: Yes. On Android devices that have **Biometric protection** set up, you can use it to secure your _**Ouisync repositories**_ and to enter _**repository**_ **Security screen**.
faqContent091102: When you set up the **biometric protection**, you will need to use it every time you want to unlock a locked _**repository**_, or when you want to enter the _**repository**_ **security** page.
faqContent091103: At present, this option is not available for desktop devices.
faqContent091104: Ouisync utilises only fingerprints – implementation of face recognition is on our roadmap.
faqTitle0912: Can I switch off the Biometric protection of the Security screen?
faqContent091201: When **Biometric protection** is **ON**, you are required to scan your fingerprint every time you want to unlock a _**repository**_ or enter into its **Security screen**.
faqContent091202: If you switch the **biometric protection OFF**, you will still be required to scan your fingerprint when you want to enter the **Security screen**.
faqContent091203: **Biometric protection** of the **Security screen** is deleted only when  **biometric protection** is removed from devices, or when you decide to use the **password** protection instead.
faqTitle1001: What are the advantages of using Ouisync over Dropbox, Google, or other similar solutions?
faqContent100102: To be able to share files using Dropbox, you need to create a Dropbox account. This requires your name, email, and credit card. It also requires payment.
faqContent100103: **Ouisync** is entirely free and open source software. To share files using **Ouisync**, you only need to install the app. That's it. No payment is required.
faqContent100105: **Ouisync** does not require the creation of user accounts. With **Ouisync**, it is simply a matter of installing the app and using it. All users are anonymous to us - we don't know who they are or where they are.
faqContent100107: This means that using **Ouisync** successfully does not depend on any central server anywhere. **Ouisync** makes use of decentralized peer-to-peer networking, which makes it an effective file-sharing app even in situations where well-known file-sharing servers (such as **Dropbox** or **Google Drive**) are unavailable.
faqContent110101: If you are using the Ouisync app on your Android phone, on top of the app **Settings** page, you will notice Connection type
faqContent110102: By default, this setting has the following orange warning message underneath: _Syncing over internet is diabled while using mobile data_.
faqContent110103: And by default, the Ouisync settings **Sync while using mobile data** is switched off.
faqContent110104: The main reason for this is to put you in control over how much mobile data you want to use for syncing the Ouisync _**repositories**_ with your peers.
faqContent110105: If you keep this setting OFF, then your Ouisync _**repositories**_ will sync with your peers only when you have access to internet or wi-fi via a router.
faqContent110106: You can also switch this setting ON.  In that case, the syncing will continue even when you are connected to internet via mobile networks.
faqContent110107: Please double check your contract with your internet provider, and keep on eye on the amount of data used, to make sure your Ouisync _**repositories**_ are not using more data than you should.
faqContent110108: In your Android Settings, the Apps section, if you tap on the Ouisync app, you will be able to see the amount of data the app used.
faqTitle1102: How does Ouisync app find my peers on the internet?
faqContent110201: To discover peers on the internet, Ouisync app uses **Bittorrent DHT** and the process of **Peer exchange**.
faqContent110202: Because there is no central server to handle requests for data upload or download, peers within the DHT will exchange the IP addresses of the peers they know about.
faqContent110203: Through these processes, your IP address may become known wthin the **Bittorrent DHT network**.  However, only the peers who possess the **Ouisync link** that you shared or that other peers shared with you, will be able to connect to your device and sync files with you.
faqTitle1103: How can I know if my device is connected to any peers?
faqContent110301: To check if your devices are connected to any peers, you can go to the app settings, section **Peers**.
faqContent110302: The small number under the word **Peers** (3 in this image) represents the number of peer devices currently connected to your device.
faqContent110303: Those peers are able to sync data with your Ouisync _**repositories**_. Each peer is able to sync data only with the _**repositories**_ you shared with them.
faqContent110304: _Example: you might have 2 _**repositories**_ - one you shared with 2 friends, and one that you shared only with your work computer._
faqcontent110305: _If they are all online at the same time, the **Peers list** on your device could show 3 peers._
faqContent110306: _Your friends won’t be able to sync data with the _**repository**_ that you ony shared with your work computer.  They are able to sync data only with _**repository**_ that they imported after you shared it with them._
faqContent110307: Tapping on **Peers** opens the **Peers screen** which displays the list of **Peers** your device is connected to, their IP addresses and their unique runtime IDs.
faqContent110308: RuntimeID is an ID that the Ouisync app generates, and [IP address](#IP) is assigned to you by your internet service provider (or sometimes router).
faqTitle1104: What happens when I switch the BitTorrentDHT and Peer exchange OFF?
faqContent110401: If these settings are switched off, Ouisync app relies on **Local discovery** protocol to find peers within your local wi-fi network or use **UPnP** to connect to other devices.
faqContent110402: **UPnP** is a service that allows devices on the  same local network to discover each other and automatically connect. This is how smart devices you may have at home connect to routers or each other.
faqContent110403: Some examples of **UPnP** devices are printers, gaming consoles, WiFi  devices, IP cameras, mobile devices, and... your Ouisync app.
faqContent110404: These settings can also be switched off.
faqTitle1105: What happens when I switch Local Discovery as well as UPnP OFF?
faqContent110501: If these settings are OFF, your Ouisync app will try to connect to your peers via internet (using the **BitTorrentDHT** and **Peer exchange** protocols.
faqContent110502: If you switch off all of the network settings (**BitTorrentDHt**, **Peer Exchange**, **Local Discovery** and the **UPnP**) you can try to connect directly to your peer's device, using their **IP address**.
faqContent110503: The following chapter will guide you through the steps you need to perform to achieve this.
faqTitle1106: How can I connect directly to my peer's device using their IP address?
faqContent110601: Although this is possible in some cases, we cannot guarantee that you will always be able to do so.
faqContent110602: To try to establish direct device-to-device connection using Ouisync app, please do the following:
faqContent110603: - Go to App Settings, and find the **Peers** item on that screen and tap on it.  In the upper right corner of the **Peers** screen you will see this button
faqContent110604: - Tapping on it will open the **User provided peers** screen.
faqContent110605: - Tapping on the plus button available on that screen will open a small window where you can enter your peer’s IP address and port numbers directly.
faqContent110606: - Your peer needs to do the same on their device at approximately the same time.  You both need to type each other’s IP address and port number in the fields provided.
faqContent110607: Once done, you can share Ouisync _**repositories**_ with that  peer.
faqContent110608: To do this, you  send them the links or QR codes in the usual way and they import those links or scan the QR codes also in the usual way.  You will then see each other’s IP address in the **Peers** screen, in the status connected, and underneath it a small label like this.
faqContent110609: If you follow these steps, the DHT will not be aware that you have shared a Ouisync _**repository**_ with a peer and it will not exchange your IP address with other peers present in the DHT lookup system.
faqContent110610: _Note: If you have previously used Ouisync or other applications that rely on BitTorrent DHT and Peer Exchange for peer discovery and data exchange (which P2P applications often do),then your IP address may be ‘remembered’ by the DHT for awhile even after you stop using those applications._
faqContent110611: _This means that If you initially share your Ouisync _**repositories**_ via internet, and you have the BitTorrent DHT ON, DHT may remember that IP address for awhile even if you then switch the BitTorrent DHT setting off and only share your Ouisync within your local network, or directly to a peer’s IP address._
faqContent110612: _This ‘remembering’ is a normal feature of the BitTorrent DHT design to speed the peer discovery within the P2P applications. If your IP address becomes inactive within the DHT (ie you don't use any applications that rely on it, for a period of time), eventually the DHT will ‘forget’ your IP address and drop it from its lookup tables._
faqTitle1107: Why can I not connect directly to my peer’s IP address?
faqContent110701: The ability of your device to connect directly to your peer’s device via their IP address depends on many things outside the Ouisync app’s control.  This could be your Internet Service provider, your device’s NAT settings, firewall settings, your router and so on. Depending on these factors, direct device-to-device connection sometimes may not be possible.
faqContent110702: Connectivity is best when your NAT type is Endpoint Independent (this is usually set by your Internet Service Provider and, as a user, you cannot change it).
faqTitle1108: Where can I find my IP address and the port number?
faqContent110801: Your IP address and your port number are available on your Ouisync app Settings screen.  Please look up the **External** IPv4 (or IPv6) entry.  Underneath it you will see both your IP address and the port number.
faqContent110802: When you want to add a peer manually, these are the numbers you need to enter into the Add peer window.
faqTitle1109: Can Ouisync app work with VPN tools?
faqContent110901: Yes.  We test the Ouisync app using VPNs and are able to sync data between devices that use VPNs and between those ones and the ones that don’t use them.
faqContent110902: However, we cannot guarantee that Ouisync app will work with absolutely every VPN solution out there.
faqContent110903: In future releases, to further increase the user’s privacy and security, we will endeavour to further annonymise the user’s IP addresses for users who don’t regularly use the VPNs.
faqTitle1110: What encryption techniques does Ouisync use?
faqContent111001: [This paper](https://github.com/equalitie/ouisync/blob/master/doc/paper.md) provides more details about inner workings of the Ouisync app. We hope you find it informative.
faqTitle1111: How can I encrypt my Ouisync data?
faqContent111101: All data that you place within the Ouisync _**repositories**_ is encrypted by the app. As a user you do not need to do anything to encrypt your data, the app does it for you.
faqContent111102: The fact that your Ouisync data is encrypted means that only people who possess the **read** or **write** link can access the Ouisync _**repositories**_.
faqTitle1112: How can I sync files with my peers or with my other devices without internet?
faqContent111201: In situations with limited internet availability, you will need to make sure some means of connecting to other devices still exists.
faqContent111202: This could be a WiFi signal available to all devices that want to share **Ouisync** _**repositories**_, or it could be intranet, a local network or similar technologies.
faqContent111203: Currently _**repositories**_ cannot be shared via Bluetooth.
faqContent120101: To install Ouisync on your Windows machine, please go to the [Microsoft App Store](https://apps.microsoft.com), type 'Ouisync' in the search box and click on the Download button.
faqContent120102: After that, in your Downloads folder, you will see the Ouisync installer.
faqContent120103: Once you run the installer, this screen will keep you informed about the installation steps that need to be performed.
faqContent120104: Once the installer runs successfully, the Ouisync app will start automatically. You can also launch it from the Start Menu.
faqContent120105: That’s it - you’ve got the Ouisync app!
faqContent120106: Upon first launching Ouisync, you will see this popup:
faqContent120201: After you start Ouisync on Windows for the first time, but before you install the Dokan library, take a look at the File Explorer and you will see something like this:
faqContent120202: After you install Dokan library, you will see something like this:
faqContent120203: Notice the O drive.  That is the Ouisync drive.  The purpose of Dokan is to mount that O drive on your Windows machine.
faqContent120204: When you create Ouisync _**repositories**_, you will see them reflected here in your File Explorer.  Without Dokan that would not be possible.
faqContent120205: With Dokan installed, you can drag and drop the files into the Ouisync _**repositories**, copy and paste, rename or delete them – all within the File Explorer.
faqContent120207: Without Dokan, Ouisync would still be able to encrypt files and sync _**repositories**_, but you would not be able to interact with your Ouisync _**repositories**_ via a file explorer.  For optimal user experience, we recommend that our users install Dokan.
faqTitle1203: Can I preview files within the Ouisync app on Windows, without Dokan?
faqContent120301: No.  They are encrypted, and within Ouisync for Windows they cannot be previewed without Dokan.
faqContent120302: If you try to view the files within the Ouisync app without having Dokan installed on your Windows machine, you will see this message at the bottom of your screen:
faqContent120303: This means that the O: drive hasn’t been mounted, so Ouisync is unable to work seamlessly with Windows File Explorer or provide a way to preview files within _**repositories**_.
faqTitle1204: Why do I see the message that my Dokan installation is out of date?
faqContent120401: If you already have Dokan installed, but you see a message that your version of Dokan is out of date, this message indicates that the version on your machine is too old to meet Ouisync's requirements.
faqContent120402: In this case, you will see a pop-up informing you of what you need to do: uninstall the existing version of Dokan, reboot your computer, and restart Ouisync.  Ouisync will then automatically begin installing the latest version of Dokan for your machine.
faqContent120403: To do this, please follow these steps.
faqContent120404: Step 1:  Stop the Ouisync app if it is running. If you simply close Ouisync by clicking on the **x** button in the upper right corner, the app will continue running in the background and you won’t be able to uninstall Dokan because the app will be trying to use it.
faqContent120405: To properly exit the app, it is necessary to go to the systems tray.
faqContent120406: Then, right click on the Ouisync icon and select Exit.
faqContent120408: To uninstall Dokan, pease go to the Windows Control Panel
faqContent120409: Then, locate Dokan in the list of Programs installed and right click > Uninstall.  Follow the onscreen prompts and, at the end of the successful uninstall, you will see this popup:
faqContent120411: Step 4: Launch the Ouisync app once more.
faqContent120412: After that, please proceed with Dokan installation.  The installer will automatically install the correct version of Dokan.
faqTitle1205: Does Ouisync launch at startup automatically?
faqContent120501: In the Ouisync app settings, we have provided an option to relaunch the Ouisync app after every computer restart.
faqContent120502: For optimal syncing with your peers, we recommend this setting be ON so that Ouisync can run in the background and receive repository changes from your peers as soon as they happen.
faqTitle1206: What are the Dokan installation steps?
faqContent120601: If needed, here’s a reminder: When you launch the Ouisync app, and you don’t have the Dokan library installed, you will see this popup:
faqContent120602: Clicking on the Install Dokan button will launch the Dokan installer. Please follow the onsreen instructions to successfully install the necessary Dokan library.
faqContent120603: After you click Finish, Dokan will be installed and Ouisync will be ready to use.
faqTitle1207: How do I use Ouisync on Windows?
faqContent120701: The Ouisync Windows app screens can be used the same way as Ouisync Android screens. Most of the frequently asked questions for the Android version of Ouisync also apply to the Windows version.
faqTitle1208: Can I create repositories using File Explorer?
faqContent120801: No. _**Repositories**_ can only be created via the Ouisync app screens.  You cannot do this using File Explorer.
faqContent120802: On your O: drive, the top level folders represent the _**repositories**_ in your Ouisync app. When Ouisync is first installed, this folder in the File Explorer will be empty.
faqContent120803: After you create a _**repository**_ within Ouisync, it will also be seen here
faqTitle1209: Can I drag and drop files into my repository using File Explorer?
faqContent120901: Yes. Once you have your _**repository**_ created, you can drag and drop files into it using the Windows File Explorer.
faqContent120902: Note: it is not possible to drag and drop files directly onto the O: drive, only into previously created _**repositories**_.
faqTitle1210: What else can I do in the File Explorer?
faqContent121001: You can use the File Explorer to add files to _**repositories**_, rename them, preview them, and delete them.
faqTitle1211: Can I manipulate repositories within the File Explorer?
faqContent121101: No.  _**Repository**_ manipulation (for example: renaming, sharing, and deleting) must be done using the Ouisync app screens.
faqTitle1301: Why do I see the red dot next to the Settings icon in my Ouisync app?
faqContent130101: Red dot represents a warning to the user that some kind of user action is needed.
faqContent130104: This red dot can be seen on the Windows version if Dokan is not installed.  See the Dokan  chapter for installation instructions.  Once Dokan is installed, the red dot disappears.
faqContent130105: If you chose to skip the Dokan installation, the red dot will persist, and you will see this message: “Accessing repositories via File Explorer is not available”.  This message and the red dot will disappear when you install Dokan.
faqContent130106: On any device - New Version is available
faqContent130107: The same red dot can also be a warning that there is a newer version of app available and that the version you have might not be able to sync the data with this newer version.
faqContent130108: This happens rarely.  Normally, if a new version of the app is available it can sync well with the previous versions.
faqContent130109: However, from time to time, we need to upgrade the protocols used for data transport.  When this happens, the app detects when two peers use different versions of protocol, and then it gives the users this warning.
faqContent130110: If you see the red dot and this warning, please install the latest version of the app.
faqTitle1302: Why do I see the orange dot next to the Settings icon in my Ouisync app?
faqContent130201: In the version 0.8.2 we have introduced a new default for the settings **sync while using mobile data**. In earlier versions the default was ON, but now it is OFF.
faqContent130202: When you want your data to sync with your peers over the internet, please change this settings to ON.
faqContent130301: Due to specific ways that Linux uses to handle the files, in this operating system, each Ouisync repository is mounted as a separate drive.
faqContent130302: Note: This is different on Windows, where the Ouisync app mounts as one O:/ drive. In Windows OS there is one Ouisync drive and each repository is repesented as a folder within the file explorer, and on Linux, there is one drive for each repository.
faqContent130303: The difference is due to different ways in which these two operating systems handle files via their respecitve file explorers.
faqContent130304: But back to Linux... When the app is closed cleanly, ie by going to the system tray and right-clicking on Exit, all these repositories are unmounted and not visible in the file explorer
faqContent130305: When the app is restarted the next time, all of them are mounted again and their contents can be viewed from the app itself as well as from the Linux file explorer.
faqContent130306: But if something unexpected happens, and the repositories are not unmounted properly, then the app may display warnings to the user:
faqContent130307: If this happens, the user might still see these repositories in the file explorer.
faqContent130308: When those warning triangles next to each repository are displayed, the repository contens are not accessible, either via the app itself or via the file epxlorer.
faqContent130309: To rectify the situation, the user needs exit the app from the system tray
faqContent130310: and then go to the file explorer, click on the ‘eject’ button next to each repository to unmount them manually.
faqContent130311: After that, they can restart the app and display and acess the repositories in the normal way.
faqContent130401: We, in the Ouisync team regularly release new versions of the app.  Our software is available in Googleplaystore, Microsoft App Store,  and soon will be in the Apple stores as well.  On top of that, we also make it available via our github repository.
faqContent130402: If you install, for example a Windows version from the Microsoft app store, and when you want to upgrade it, you download a new version from our github repository... Your upgrade is not going to work.
faqContent130403: You will, in effect, have two different Ouisync apps running on your windows machine and this can give rise to some conflicts.
faqContent130404: The reason for this is that each platform signs the software they distribute, and because of these different signatures, the operating system will treat the two versions as two different apps.
faqContent130405: There will be two Ouisync icons on your desktop, and the two instances will be able to run in parallel which can give rise to some conflicts. If that happens, please uninstall the ‘upgraded’ version, and run the upgrade from the same platform where the original app version was installed from.
faqContent130406: Equally, if you first install the your Android Ouisync app from the Google play store, and then you want to install a version from the github, the Android installation will fail.
faqContent130407: To be able to upgrade the Ouisync app correctly, we recommend to always use the software from the platform that you used for your first installation.
faqTitle1305: What happens to my Ouisync data if I uninstall the app?
faqContent130501: If you uninstall your Ouisync app on Android, the data you had in your Ouisync repositories will also be deleted.  If you have shared your repositories with another device or a peer, they will be able to generate the QR code or link for you and you’ll be able to reimport the same repositories.
faqContent130502: On Windows, uninstalling Ouisync app  by going to Programs > Uninstall does not delete the previously existing repositories.  If you wish to delete them, you need to do that by going to the file explorer, finding them on your hard drive, and deleting them from there.
faqTitle1306: My file explorer doesn’t display all repositories on Windows (or Linux) - what can I check?
faqContent130601: - Please verify that the repository that you want to access via file explorer is unlocked. Locked repositories may not be displayed or may not be accessible via file explorer on Windows or Linux machines.
faqContent130602: - Please check what access right you have for each repository.  Blind repositories may not be displayed in file exporer, but read repositories will allow you to view the files they contain and write will allow you to add, delete, rename or move files.
faqTitle1307: I cannot see my repositories in the file explorer in my Windows computer - what can I do?
faqContent130701: If you have installed both the Ouisync app and the Dokan software that the app needs, and you still cannot see your repositories in the File explorer, please try to reboot your Windows machine.  Sometimes this is necessary for the Dokan parameters to be taken into account by the Windows operating system.
